title: Add weather area overlays: precipitation radar, cloud cover map, and pressure system zones
description: Transform the weather visualization from point-based indicators to rich area overlays on the map. Uses RainViewer for real precipitation radar data and a grid-based approach with Open-Meteo for cloud cover and pressure system rendering.

plan.summary:
Add three weather area overlay layers to the Leaflet map:

1. **Precipitation radar** — integrate RainViewer's free radar tile API to show actual radar imagery as a colored Leaflet tile layer (green→yellow→red for intensity). No backend changes needed — purely client-side tile layer.

2. **Cloud cover areas** — fetch weather data for a 5×5 grid of points (~50km spacing) around the boat from a new backend endpoint, then render semi-transparent white/gray radial gradient blobs on a dedicated Leaflet canvas overlay, sized and positioned by lat/lon. Each blob's opacity scales with cloud_cover percentage.

3. **Pressure system zones** — use the same grid data to render colored gradient zones on the map. Blue for high pressure (≥1020 hPa), red for low pressure (≤1005 hPa), with H/L labels at extremes. Rendered as a Leaflet canvas overlay with radial gradients.

Open-Meteo supports multi-location queries (comma-separated lat/lon), so the grid fetch is a single API call for all ~25 points — requesting only cloud_cover and pressure_msl to keep it fast.

New layer toggles ('Radar', 'Cloud Map', 'Pressure Map') will be added to the existing Layers panel so users can toggle each overlay independently. The existing particle-based precipitation, cloud dimming, and pressure badge remain as complementary visualizations.

The main JS modification approach: add new code sections to the stored JS in _temp_build (new functions for RainViewer, grid fetch, cloud/pressure rendering, and layer setup). The existing particle system and indicator badges are untouched.

steps.description: Step 1: Create the `/api/weather-grid` backend endpoint — a new handler `getWeatherGrid` and shared function `fetchWeatherGrid`. The function takes center lat/lon, computes a 5×5 grid of coordinates (~0.5° spacing ≈ 55km), and calls Open-Meteo with all 25 lat/lon pairs in a single request (comma-separated), requesting only `cloud_cover` and `pressure_msl` for current+hourly. Returns an array of {lat, lon, cloudCover, pressure} objects. The handler validates coords using the existing `withValidatedCoords` helper and returns the grid data. (Reqs 2, 3)

Step 2: Add RainViewer precipitation radar integration to the client JS. New functions: `initRainViewer()` fetches the RainViewer maps API (https://api.rainviewer.com/public/weather-maps.json) to get available radar timestamps, creates a Leaflet tile layer using the latest radar frame URL, and adds it to the map with ~0.4 opacity. New `rainRadar` layer variable tracks the tile layer. Called during initialization after initMap(). Toggled by the new 'radar' layer checkbox. (Req 1)

Step 3: Add cloud cover area rendering to the client JS. New function `fetchAndRenderGridOverlays()` calls `/api/weather-grid`, then `renderCloudAreas(gridData)` creates a Leaflet canvas overlay that draws white radial gradient circles at each grid point's map position. Each circle's opacity scales with cloudCover (0% = invisible, 100% = 0.35 alpha). The overlay hooks into map zoom/pan to reposition circles. Updates on data refresh and timeline scrub (using hourly grid data). Toggled by new 'cloudMap' layer checkbox. (Req 2)

Step 4: Add pressure system zone rendering to the client JS. New function `renderPressureAreas(gridData)` creates a Leaflet canvas overlay that draws colored radial gradients at each grid point: blue for high pressure (≥1015 hPa, intensity scales above 1015), red/orange for low pressure (≤1015 hPa, intensity scales below 1015). At local extremes (highest/lowest pressure in the grid), render bold 'H' or 'L' labels. Toggled by new 'pressureMap' layer checkbox. (Req 3)

Step 5: Update the HTML template to add 3 new layer toggle checkboxes to the Layers panel: 'Radar' (data-layer='radar'), 'Cloud Map' (data-layer='cloudMap'), 'Pressure Map' (data-layer='pressureMap'). Add them in a visually separated 'Overlays' subsection below the existing particle toggles. (Reqs 1, 2, 3)

Step 6: Update the JS layer toggle handler to recognize the 3 new layer keys. When 'radar' toggles, show/hide the RainViewer tile layer. When 'cloudMap' toggles, show/hide the cloud canvas overlay. When 'pressureMap' toggles, show/hide the pressure canvas overlay. Wire up grid fetch on initial load and on significant map movement. (Reqs 1, 2, 3)

Step 7: Add CSS for the new overlays — style the canvas overlays with proper z-index and pointer-events:none so they don't block map interaction. Style the 'Overlays' subsection separator in the layer panel. (Reqs 1, 2, 3)

Step 8: Write tests for the new `/api/weather-grid` endpoint — verify it returns valid grid data with lat, lon, cloudCover, pressure fields. Update existing tests if needed. Run all tests and check_export_types. Take a screenshot to visually verify the overlays. (All reqs)
